source.subject: Quick walkthrough & alternate options after your trial signup
messages:
From: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Zhao <alex@pivotalbio.com>
Date: Fri, 30 Aug 2029 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Quick walkthrough & alternate options after your trial signup

Hi Alex —

Thanks for signing up for the self-serve trial! I pulled your account and saw you had a walkthrough slot booked earlier today but we missed you. Totally understand — these things happen. A few quick options so we can get you unstuck without creating extra overhead on your side: 

1) Live demo (20 min): I can reschedule a short, focused walkthrough — pick any 15–20 minute slot here: https://cal.redwood.ai/s/vanessa-15min
2) Async walkthrough (3-min annotated video): Watch our short overview and I’ll follow up with tailored next steps: https://redwood.ai/async-walkthroughs/overview-3min
3) Drop-in office hours: We hold 15‑minute drop-in windows Wed/Thu 8–10 AM PT — jump in anytime, no pre-scheduling needed.

If you prefer, I can also have a solutions engineer join for a 15‑minute compliance/IT-focused check. I attached a one-page quick guide that highlights common gotchas for trial environments.

Which option works best for you? If time is tight, reply with ASN (async + short drop-in) and I’ll send the links and checklist.

Best,
Vanessa Ortiz
SDR, Redwood Inference
vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0148
Attachment: redwood-quick-guide.pdf

From: Alex Zhao <alex@pivotalbio.com>
To: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>
Cc: Maya Singh <maya.singh@pivotalbio.com>
Date: Fri, 30 Aug 2029 09:35:22 -0700
Subject: Re: Quick walkthrough & alternate options after your trial signup

Hi Vanessa —

Sorry about missing the slot this morning — got pulled into an all-hands. Appreciate the flexible options. For our team the fastest path is:

- Async walkthrough first (I can watch it on the plane later)
- Then one 15‑minute drop-in where I can bring our infra lead (Maya) to confirm connectivity and data handling.

Can you also send a short checklist I can forward to our IT folks (firewall / allowed domains / certs)? If you have SOC2 evidence or an on-demand compliance doc, please include that as well — our procurement will ask.

Thanks — I’ll watch the video tonight.

— Alex
Senior Product Manager, Pivotal Bio

From: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Zhao <alex@pivotalbio.com>
Cc: Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 30 Aug 2029 10:05:14 -0700
Subject: Re: Quick walkthrough & alternate options after your trial signup

Great — thanks for the quick reply, Alex.

I’ve done the two things you asked for:
- Async walkthrough: https://redwood.ai/async-walkthroughs/overview-3min (short annotated video)
- Drop-in hours: Wed/Thu 8–10 AM PT — just drop in and we’ll pick up from there.

I’ve looped in Mina from Solutions (cc’d) and asked her to share the IT/ connectivity checklist and the on-demand compliance links. Mina — would you please attach the connectivity checklist and SOC2 pointer we can share?

If you prefer a scheduled 15‑minute session instead of drop-in, let me know a couple windows and I’ll lock it.

Thanks,
Vanessa
Attachment: redwood-quick-guide.pdf

From: Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Zhao <alex@pivotalbio.com>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 30 Aug 2029 11:22:40 -0700
Subject: Re: Quick walkthrough & alternate options after your trial signup

Hi Alex —

Happy to help. I attached a short connectivity checklist and included the most common items our IT teams ask about:

• Outbound domains to allow: api.redwood.ai, auth.redwood.ai
• Ports: 443 (TLS) outbound; no inbound required for trial
• IP ranges: we publish a stable CIDR block for Dedicated — for hosted trials, just allow the domains above
• Token handling: we recommend short-lived service tokens for test environments; avoid embedding long-lived keys in shared images

SOC2 & compliance: https://drive.redwood.ai/shared/soc2-report (view-only link)

Attachment: connectivity-checklist.xlsx

I’ll plan to join drop-in hours tomorrow morning in case you or Maya pop in — if you’d rather book a dedicated 15 minute slot, reply with 2 windows and I’ll confirm.

Regards,
Mina Haddad
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.ai>
To: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 30 Aug 2029 15:02:00 -0700
Subject: Internal: Covering drop-in hours

Vanessa — quick internal note. I’ve added an internal calendar event "Redwood - Alex Zhao (Pivotal Bio) - 15m drop-in" on tomorrow 08:30–08:45 PT and set a reminder for Mina and myself. We’ll be online to help with connectivity questions if they join. No external recipients on this one — just flagging so we have coverage.

Thanks,
Ethan
Engineering Ops, Redwood Inference

source.subject: Targeted region surge — immediate traffic steering advice & recovery steps
messages:
From: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.ai>
To: Acme AI <infra@acme.ai>, Morgan Chen <morgan@datasurge.com>
Cc: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 02 Oct 2028 08:03:00 -0700
Subject: Targeted region surge — immediate traffic steering advice & recovery steps

Hi Morgan / Acme infra team —

We’re seeing a targeted capacity/egress degradation affecting requests routed to eu-west-1 and ap-southeast-1 in the last 45 minutes. Symptoms we observe: elevated 5xx rate (predominantly 502/504), increased p99 latency for generation calls, and intermittent KV fetch timeouts for a subset of hosts. Impact is limited to a subset of customers behind specific cloud load balancer paths; US regions are nominal right now.

Immediate mitigation steps (what you can do right now):
- Prefer region override: set inference.region=us-west-2 or us-east-1 in your Redwood client to avoid eu/apac endpoints for the next 2–4 hours.
- Enable fallback model: if you have model_fallback configured, temporarily permit "redwood-compact-v1" as a lower-latency fallback to reduce retries.
- Increase client timeout to 30s (was 15s) and retry with exponential backoff (max 2 retries) to avoid duplicate completions.
- Avoid large batch windows; switch to smaller batch_size (<=8) to reduce queueing pressure.

We’re pushing a targeted traffic-steer at our edge to move new connections away from the affected POPs, but some in-flight sessions may still hit degraded hosts — the region override is the fastest customer-facing control.

Attachments: incident-summary-20281002.pdf (short summary) and a small trace snapshot for affected APAC hosts.

I’ll follow up with ETA and root-cause once the mitigation stabilizes.

—Camila
Camila Reyes
Account Lead, Americas
Redwood Inference
From: Morgan Chen <morgan@datasurge.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.ai>, Acme AI <infra@acme.ai>
Cc: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 02 Oct 2028 09:12:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Targeted region surge — immediate traffic steering advice & recovery steps

Thanks Camila — confirmation from our side: we started seeing 502 spikes at 08:18 PDT for customers with eu-west-1 affinity. We pushed region override to a subset of services and are seeing fewer 5xx but still occasional timeouts on long-polling endpoints.

Quick question: will this steering change affect billing (requests counted against fallback model)? Also, can you confirm if cached embeddings will still be served from cache hits (we rely on a heavy embedding cache layer)?

Morgan
Site Reliability Lead, DataSurge (Acme AI partner)
From: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwood.ai>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.ai>, Morgan Chen <morgan@datasurge.com>, Acme AI <infra@acme.ai>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 02 Oct 2028 09:34:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Targeted region surge — immediate traffic steering advice & recovery steps

Morgan —
Short answers:
- Billing: routing to a fallback model variant counts per-token on the model variant used. If you enable redwood-compact-v1 as fallback, tokens consumed there will be billed at that variant’s rate. We can work a temporary credit if this materially impacts you; Camila will handle credits.
- Embedding cache: prefix/KV cache hits remain served from the cache if the cache layer is local to the application or via our global CDN. If your cache lookup is a separate request that would have hit apac/eu, steering away means you’ll see cache-miss patterns only if cache was region-bound.

Operational notes from our side: we’re performing a micro-flow drain of 3 edge hosts in eu-west-1 and 2 hosts in ap-southeast-1 showing abnormal TCP retransmit rates; new sessions are being diverted to us-east-1/us-west-2. Expect gradual recovery in 30–90 minutes as hosts rejoin rotation.

For now: set timeouts, enable fallback, and reduce batch size. We’ll provide a public status update in 15 minutes with the incident ID.

—Ethan
Ethan Park
Senior Incident Engineer, Redwood Inference
From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.ai>, Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwood.ai>, Morgan Chen <morgan@datasurge.com>
Date: Tue, 02 Oct 2028 10:08:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Targeted region surge — immediate traffic steering advice & recovery steps

Adding technical detail / small remediation steps for platform engineers at Acme: 
- DNS TTL: if you control a short client-side DNS TTL, keep it <= 60s for the next hour to ensure clients pick up new edge IPs.
- Connection draining: set keepalive to 10s and ensure stale connections are closed quickly to avoid hitting degraded sockets.
- Retry idempotency: add request-id headers (x-rw-request-id) so we can dedupe retries if a retry does succeed after a delayed completion.

Attached: edge-trace-apac-eu.txt — 3 sample traces (headers anonymized) showing where we observed retransmits and the host IDs.

I’ll follow up with the exact host IDs and the drain schedule.

—Marcus
Marcus Lin
Platform Reliability Lead, Redwood Inference
From: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.ai>
To: Acme AI <infra@acme.ai>, Morgan Chen <morgan@datasurge.com>
Cc: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 02 Oct 2028 17:42:00 +0000
Subject: Incident wrap + next steps and postmortem cadence — Targeted region surge (eu/apac)

Hi Morgan / Acme infra team,

Quick update: hosts drained and traffic-steer in place; 5xx rate has dropped to baseline for your routed accounts. We’ll keep the steering active until the cloud provider completes the infrastructure patch for the affected hosts (estimated 3–6 hours from now).

Immediate follow-ups we are committing to:
- Formal postmortem (shared draft) by 2028-10-04 — leads: Ethan + Marcus.
- Short customer-facing timeline and root-cause summary by 2028-10-03 18:00 UTC.
- Compensation: Camila will open a support credit request for impacted accounts (Morgan, I’ll include Acme’s usage window).
- Action: we recommend adding a regional-fallback policy on your account settings to allow automatic steering during platform-targeted degradations — I’ll send a short how-to after the postmortem.

Thank you for the rapid coordination — please reply here if you see regressions. We’ll also publish the incident page at the link below once the postmortem is posted.

Status link: https://status.redwood.ai/incidents/2028-10-02-rls-77
JIRA: INF-4592 (postmortem)

—Camila
Camila Reyes
Account Lead, Americas
Redwood Inference

To: Acme AI <infra@acme.ai>, Morgan Chen <morgan@datasurge.com>
Cc: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 02 Oct 2028 08:03:00 -0700
Subject: Targeted region surge — immediate traffic steering advice & recovery steps

Hi Morgan / Acme infra team —

We’re seeing a targeted capacity/egress degradation affecting requests routed to eu-west-1 and ap-southeast-1 in the last 45 minutes. Symptoms we observe: elevated 5xx rate (predominantly 502/504), increased p99 latency for generation calls, and intermittent KV fetch timeouts for a subset of hosts. Impact is limited to a subset of customers behind specific cloud load balancer paths; US regions are nominal right now.

Immediate mitigation steps (what you can do right now):
- Prefer region override: set inference.region=us-west-2 or us-east-1 in your Redwood client to avoid eu/apac endpoints for the next 2–4 hours.
- Enable fallback model: if you have model_fallback configured, temporarily permit "redwood-compact-v1" as a lower-latency fallback to reduce retries.
- Increase client timeout to 30s (was 15s) and retry with exponential backoff (max 2 retries) to avoid duplicate completions.
- Avoid large batch windows; switch to smaller batch_size (<=8) to reduce queueing pressure.

We’re pushing a targeted traffic-steer at our edge to move new connections away from the affected POPs, but some in-flight sessions may still hit degraded hosts — the region override is the fastest customer-facing control.

Attachments: incident-summary-20281002.pdf (short summary) and a small trace snapshot for affected APAC hosts.

I’ll follow up with ETA and root-cause once the mitigation stabilizes.

—Camila
Camila Reyes
Account Lead, Americas
Redwood Inference


From: Morgan Chen <morgan@datasurge.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.ai>, Acme AI <infra@acme.ai>
Cc: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 02 Oct 2028 09:12:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Targeted region surge — immediate traffic steering advice & recovery steps

Thanks Camila — confirmation from our side: we started seeing 502 spikes at 08:18 PDT for customers with eu-west-1 affinity. We pushed region override to a subset of services and are seeing fewer 5xx but still occasional timeouts on long-polling endpoints.

Quick question: will this steering change affect billing (requests counted against fallback model)? Also, can you confirm if cached embeddings will still be served from cache hits (we rely on a heavy embedding cache layer)?

Morgan
Site Reliability Lead, DataSurge (Acme AI partner)


From: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwood.ai>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.ai>, Morgan Chen <morgan@datasurge.com>, Acme AI <infra@acme.ai>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 02 Oct 2028 09:34:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Targeted region surge — immediate traffic steering advice & recovery steps

Morgan —
Short answers:
- Billing: routing to a fallback model variant counts per-token on the model variant used. If you enable redwood-compact-v1 as fallback, tokens consumed there will be billed at that variant’s rate. We can work a temporary credit if this materially impacts you; Camila will handle credits.
- Embedding cache: prefix/KV cache hits remain served from the cache if the cache layer is local to the application or via our global CDN. If your cache lookup is a separate request that would have hit apac/eu, steering away means you’ll see cache-miss patterns only if cache was region-bound.

Operational notes from our side: we’re performing a micro-flow drain of 3 edge hosts in eu-west-1 and 2 hosts in ap-southeast-1 showing abnormal TCP retransmit rates; new sessions are being diverted to us-east-1/us-west-2. Expect gradual recovery in 30–90 minutes as hosts rejoin rotation.

For now: set timeouts, enable fallback, and reduce batch size. We’ll provide a public status update in 15 minutes with the incident ID.

—Ethan
Ethan Park
Senior Incident Engineer, Redwood Inference


From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>
To: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.ai>, Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwood.ai>, Morgan Chen <morgan@datasurge.com>
Date: Tue, 02 Oct 2028 10:08:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Targeted region surge — immediate traffic steering advice & recovery steps

Adding technical detail / small remediation steps for platform engineers at Acme: 
- DNS TTL: if you control a short client-side DNS TTL, keep it <= 60s for the next hour to ensure clients pick up new edge IPs.
- Connection draining: set keepalive to 10s and ensure stale connections are closed quickly to avoid hitting degraded sockets.
- Retry idempotency: add request-id headers (x-rw-request-id) so we can dedupe retries if a retry does succeed after a delayed completion.

Attached: edge-trace-apac-eu.txt — 3 sample traces (headers anonymized) showing where we observed retransmits and the host IDs.

I’ll follow up with the exact host IDs and the drain schedule.

—Marcus
Marcus Lin
Platform Reliability Lead, Redwood Inference


From: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.ai>
To: Acme AI <infra@acme.ai>, Morgan Chen <morgan@datasurge.com>
Cc: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 02 Oct 2028 17:42:00 +0000
Subject: Incident wrap + next steps and postmortem cadence — Targeted region surge (eu/apac)

Hi Morgan / Acme infra team,

Quick update: hosts drained and traffic-steer in place; 5xx rate has dropped to baseline for your routed accounts. We’ll keep the steering active until the cloud provider completes the infrastructure patch for the affected hosts (estimated 3–6 hours from now).

Immediate follow-ups we are committing to:
- Formal postmortem (shared draft) by 2028-10-04 — leads: Ethan + Marcus.
- Short customer-facing timeline and root-cause summary by 2028-10-03 18:00 UTC.
- Compensation: Camila will open a support credit request for impacted accounts (Morgan, I’ll include Acme’s usage window).
- Action: we recommend adding a regional-fallback policy on your account settings to allow automatic steering during platform-targeted degradations — I’ll send a short how-to after the postmortem.

Thank you for the rapid coordination — please reply here if you see regressions. We’ll also publish the incident page at the link below once the postmortem is posted.

Status link: https://status.redwood.ai/incidents/2028-10-02-rls-77
JIRA: INF-4592 (postmortem)

—Camila
Camila Reyes
Account Lead, Americas
Redwood Inference

source.subject: Interleaved demo shift plan & proposed overlap matrix
messages:
From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.ai>
To: Karen Liu <karen.liu@globex.com>
Cc: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.ai>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-07-29T16:03:00Z
Subject: Interleaved demo shift plan & proposed overlap matrix

Hi Karen —

Thanks again for the time yesterday. Per our conversation, it sounds like we need a demo cadence that hits hands-off windows for Globex's platform engineers in APAC, EU, and the US, and also a follow-up slot for procurement/security to validate the scope and SLAs. To avoid forcing any one region into very late hours, I put together a short proposal (attached) with a 3-shift interleaved plan and a matrix of overlap windows.

Quick summary of the proposal in the sheet:
- Three recurring demo slots that rotate each week so that no region is always the after-hours one.
- A dedicated 30-min security/procurement check immediately after one of the demo slots each week (recorded for the team).
- Hand-off notes template so engineers can pick up asynchronous items.

Can you confirm whether the engineers prefer the first week to prioritize APAC-friendly times (we can start the rotation there), or start with Europe to match your legal team's availability? I also attached a simple availability grid — if you prefer, pick a slot via my Calendly link below and I’ll lock the invites.

Attachments: availability-grid.xlsx, timezones-overlap.png
Links: https://calendly.com/ethan-park/redwood-demo-30

Thanks,
Ethan Park
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ethan.park@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0132

---

Note: we’ll loop in procurement@globex.com once we have a weekly recurring pattern to confirm terms and the security pre-check that Karen mentioned.
From: Karen Liu <karen.liu@globex.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.ai>
Cc: Rajiv Menon <rajiv@globex.com>, procurement@globex.com
Date: 2028-07-30T09:22:00+02:00
Subject: Re: Interleaved demo shift plan & proposed overlap matrix

Thanks Ethan — this looks promising. A few notes:

1) Engineering availability: APAC (India/Japan) team prefers afternoons local time, EU prefers mornings (CEST), and our US SREs are typically available late-afternoon PT.
2) Procurement: procurement@globex.com wants the security overview documented and a 30-min slot with our legal & info-sec once per month.
3) SOC2 evidence: our security team will ask for the SOC2 attestation and a list of data flows; can Soojin handle a short run-through in the first session?

If you can propose two concrete recurring schedules (one starting rotation on APAC, one starting on EU) we’ll pick the one that minimizes travel for senior engineers. Also, can you include an explicit hand-off checklist to capture outstanding action items?

Karen Liu
Principal Engineering Manager, Globex Cloud
karen.liu@globex.com

On Jul 29, 2028, at 9:03 AM, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.ai> wrote:
> (attachment: availability-grid.xlsx)

From: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.ai>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.ai>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Cc: Karen Liu <karen.liu@globex.com>, Rajiv Menon <rajiv@globex.com>
Date: 2028-07-30T17:10:00Z
Subject: Re: Interleaved demo shift plan & proposed overlap matrix

+1 on the checklist. I drafted a compact hand-off template and annotated the matrix with expected latency/throughput examples for the demo scenarios (see attached image). For the SOC2 walk-through: I can cover the high-level controls in the first demo and leave a 15–20 minute slot for evidence requests.

Proposed concrete schedules (all times shown as local start -> UTC):
Option A — start rotation in APAC week (preferred by customer):
- Week 1 (APAC-priority): 09:30 JST / 02:30 UTC (APAC-friendly) — overlaps EU by 30 mins; US joins late (record session for US).
- Week 2 (EU-priority): 10:00 CEST / 08:00 UTC — overlaps US and APAC lightly.
- Week 3 (US-priority): 15:00 PT / 22:00 UTC — US-friendly, APAC on early morning next day for handoff.

Option B — start rotation in EU week:
- Week 1 (EU-priority): 09:00 CEST / 07:00 UTC
- Week 2 (US-priority): 14:00 PT / 21:00 UTC
- Week 3 (APAC-priority): 10:00 JST / 01:00 UTC

I recommend Option A — it keeps the first-week momentum in APAC and gives procurement a predictable recorded slot shortly thereafter. I’ll prepare the calendar series and the hand-off template if you want me to.

Soojin Lee
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
soojin.lee@redwood.ai

From: Rajiv Menon <rajiv@globex.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.ai>, Karen Liu <karen.liu@globex.com>
Cc: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.ai>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, procurement@globex.com
Date: 2028-07-31T11:22:00+02:00
Subject: Re: Interleaved demo shift plan & proposed overlap matrix

On Mon, Jul 31, 2028 at 11:22 AM GMT+2, Karen Liu <karen.liu@globex.com> wrote:
> Thanks Ethan — this looks promising. A few notes:
>
> 1) Engineering availability: APAC (India/Japan) team prefers afternoons local time, EU prefers mornings (CEST), and our US SREs are typically available late-afternoon PT.

Ethan / Soojin — Option A works for us. One operational request from SREs: could we alternate the 15-min pre-demo sync so our on-call is not repeatedly in odd hours? If rotation is weekly, that should distribute the impact. Also, please include a short summary line at the top of calendar invites with the precise objectives for that session (e.g., \"demo: inference latency at 512 tokens / BAU integration checklist\").

Procurement: we’ll confirm the monthly security slot once your team shares the recorded session cadence.

Thanks,
Rajiv
Senior Platform Engineer, Globex Cloud
rajiv@globex.com
From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.ai>
To: Karen Liu <karen.liu@globex.com>, Rajiv Menon <rajiv@globex.com>
Cc: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.ai>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, procurement@globex.com
Date: 2028-08-01T09:18:00Z
Subject: Re: Interleaved demo shift plan & proposed overlap matrix

Thanks everyone — great call-out on alternating the pre-demo sync. Finalizing per your requests:

- We will run Option A. Rotation frequency: weekly. Pre-demo sync will rotate among on-call engineers (15 min) to avoid repetitive late hours.
- Weekly recurring calendar series created (invites sent) with the first live session on 2028-08-07 at 09:30 JST (APAC-priority week). Invite includes the session objective and the hand-off checklist.
- Soojin will cover the SOC2 overview in the first session and attach the SOC2 summary and data flow diagram to the calendar invite.
- Procurement: we created a monthly 30-min security slot on the first Tuesday of each month after the APAC session; procurement@globex.com is on the invite.
- Attachments uploaded: availability-grid.xlsx, timezones-overlap.png. HubSpot deal updated: HS-52341.

Please reply if any conflicts remain; otherwise we’ll treat the invites as confirmed.

Best,
Ethan Park
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ethan.park@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0132

(attachments: availability-grid.xlsx, timezones-overlap.png)

From: Emma Park <emma@skyline.ai>
To: Irene Choi <irene@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 30 Jul 2028 09:24:00 -0700
Subject: Skyline HQ immersion — need to shift date

Hi Irene,

Quick note: our executive sponsor just had a travel conflict and asks if we can move the onsite immersion originally slated for Thu Aug 8 to one of the following: Mon Aug 5 (morning), Thu Aug 10 (anytime after 09:00), or Fri Aug 11 (morning).

We expect 6 attendees from Skyline (engineering + product + one exec). Can you confirm which of those dates works for Redwood? Also: what do you need from us for NDA/badging and parking? I can send badges in advance if you prefer.

I've attached a draft Skyline agenda we were planning to follow — small tweaks likely.

Thanks,
Emma
Emma Park
Head of Product Partnerships | Skyline AI
emma@skyline.ai

Attachment: Skyline-Agenda-v1.xlsx (spreadsheet stub)

From: Irene Choi <irene@redwood.ai>
To: Emma Park <emma@skyline.ai>
Cc: Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.ai>, Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 30 Jul 2028 10:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Skyline HQ immersion — need to shift date

Hi Emma — thanks for the heads up.

Good news: Thu Aug 10 looks best on our side. We can run 09:30–14:00 and still accommodate the hands-on demo benches you mentioned. If that works for you we'll confirm the meeting room and AV and send a finalized agenda.

Requests from our side so we can streamline check-in:
- Final headcount + full names by end of day Wed Aug 2
- Completed Visitor Badging Form for each guest (name, DOB, photo ID type) — we can pre-print badges if you send the forms
- Any special dietary needs for the light lunch (we'll provide boxed lunches)

Looping in Ethan (ops) for room/AV and Grace for team sync on demo scope. We'll also attach a Redwood draft agenda for alignment.

If you prefer Aug 5 instead, let me know and we'll re-check room availability; Aug 11 is tricky for our execs.

Best,
Irene Choi
Strategic Partnerships | Redwood Inference
irene@redwood.ai

Attachment: Redwood-Immersion-Draft.pdf (agenda stub)

From: Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.ai>
To: Irene Choi <irene@redwood.ai>, Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 30 Jul 2028 11:17:00 -07:00

Logistics notes for Emma's team if we confirm Aug 10:
- Visitor badging: send completed Visitor-Badging-Form PDFs to ops@redwood.ai by EOD Aug 7. We'll pre-print badges and have an escort at reception.
- Parking: we can reserve 3 visitor passes in the west lot; guests should display the pass or use the QR for gate entry.
- Catering: boxed lunch options (vegetarian, gluten-free, chicken). Please provide any allergies.
- Demo benches: we'll allocate 2 benches with power + 1 high-performance laptop for performance checks (let me know if you need GPUs on-site).

-Ethan

Facilities & Events | Redwood Inference
ethan@redwood.ai

Subject: Re: Skyline HQ immersion — need to shift date

source.subject: CobaltMetrics: AZ2 throughput & 5xx surge — urgent mitigation and exec sync
messages:
From: Elena Costa <elena.costa@cobaltmetrics.com>
To: support@redwood.com
Date: Tue, 25 Jul 2028 08:12:00 -0700
Subject: High latency + 502 spike in us-west-az2 (production)

Hi Redwood team,

We're seeing a sharp increase in tail latency across our production traffic (started ~07:50 PT) and a spike of 502/503 responses from instances in us-west-az2. Throughput has collapsed from our normal 1.2k rps to ~350 rps and a subset of requests are timing out at the 10s gateway. Sample logs attached (cobaltmetrics-sample-502s.log).

This is impacting our live dashboard and partner integrations. Can someone confirm whether this is an AZ-localized hardware/instance problem or a broader regional routing issue? We need immediate mitigation — either a cross-region drain or an emergency capacity bump.

Elena
Elena Costa
Head of Platform, CobaltMetrics
elena.costa@cobaltmetrics.com
+1 (415) 555-0198

---
Attachments: cobaltmetrics-sample-502s.log (text/log, 48 KB)

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Hannah Schmitt <hannah.schmitt@redwood.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 25 Jul 2028 09:05:12 -0700
Subject: Fwd: High latency + 502 spike in us-west-az2 (production) — urgent

Looping the on-call infra and routing leads. Elena — thanks for the logs. We saw correlated 5xx amplification and a token-cache pressure pattern in us-west-az2 around the same time window.

Immediate asks:
- Hannah / infra: confirm AZ-level capacity / instance health and whether any autoscaler anomalies were observed.
- Soojin / routing: evaluate a temporary cross-region steer (us-west-az2 -> us-west-az1? OR eastus failover) and estimate TTL for DNS/edge reweighting.
- Marissa / CS: keep customer comms tight; tell them we're treating this as a P1 and will provide a timeline.

I'll escalate to execs if we need a manual global re-weight or SLA exception (credits/priority capacity). Please reply with play-by-play and propose one recommended mitigation by 09:30 PT.

Marcus
Marcus Lin
Senior Customer Reliability Manager, Redwood Inference
marcus.lin@redwood.com
---
Attachments: az2-traffic-profile.csv (csv, 22 KB)

From: Hannah Schmitt <hannah.schmitt@redwood.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 25 Jul 2028 09:26:47 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: High latency + 502 spike in us-west-az2 (production) — urgent

Marcus / team — initial findings:
- Instance health: host-level metrics show no immediate machine-level failure (EC2/hyperscaler reports OK).
- KV cache miss amplification: a subset of large multi-turn workloads exhausted local KV shards and caused high tail-latency while autoscaler spun up replacements. This produced request retries which magnified 502s.
- Autoscaler: increased nodes but observed slow replenishment (~6–8 min to restore pre-surge capacity) due to image pull / cold start for the large model variant.

Recommendation (one-step mitigation):
1) Immediate routing policy change: divert 60% of traffic from us-west-az2 to us-west-az1 and 40% to eastus2 for low-latency preservation. This avoids a full region failover and keeps most traffic within US latency SLOs. Estimated customer-perceived impact: brief connection resets for long-lived streams (~5–15s) as we drain.
2) Concurrent: spin a warm pool of model-backed instances with pre-warmed images (manual burst) in us-west-az2 to stabilise KV pressure. This requires exec approval for temporary capacity surge (expected 8 GPUs, ~2 hour window).

I can implement the routing weights now if Marcus approves. Playbook reference: manual-routing-playbook.pdf (excerpt attached).

Hannah
Hannah Schmitt
Principal SRE, Redwood Inference
hannah.schmitt@redwood.com
---
Attachments: manual-routing-playbook.pdf (pdf, 120 KB)

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Elena Costa <elena.costa@cobaltmetrics.com>, support@cobaltmetrics.com
Cc: Hannah Schmitt <hannah.schmitt@redwood.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 25 Jul 2028 18:33:05 -07:00
Subject: RE: High latency + 502 spike in us-west-az2 (production) — mitigation and next steps

Elena — quick update and actions we've taken today. Thanks again for the sample logs and patience.

What we did (timeline):
- 09:30 PT: Implemented weighted routing (60% -> us-west-az1, 40% -> eastus2) to reduce load on us-west-az2 and prevent global retry amplification.
- 09:35 PT: Activated pre-warmed instance pool in us-west-az2 (8 GPUs) to address KV cache pressure; observed gradual reduction in tail latency over the next hour.
- 11:15 PT: Observed stable throughput recovering to ~95% of baseline; 5xx rate reduced to <0.5% over 1 hour.

Current status: throughput and error rates are back within normal bounds for the last 4 hours. We continue to monitor for KV miss patterns during peak windows and will keep the warm pool active through tomorrow's peak.

Next steps (customer-visible & internal):
- We'll keep weighted routing in place for the rest of the day and begin a controlled return to original weights at 0200 PT overnight.
- We'll run an RCA and share an executive summary by EOD tomorrow.
- We propose a one-time service credit for the incident (we will surface the proposed credit amount separately) and can open a dedicated post-incident bridge if you'd like further granularity.

If you'd like an exec-to-exec update, Ethan Park (cc'd) is available this afternoon for a 20-min sync.

Elena, please let us know if you need additional logs or want the bridge recording. We're sorry for the impact and appreciate the quick flag.

Marcus
Marcus Lin
Senior Customer Reliability Manager, Redwood Inference
marcus.lin@redwood.com
---
Attachments: none

To: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 25 Jul 2028 09:26:47 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: High latency + 502 spike in us-west-az2 (production) — urgent

Marcus / team — initial findings:
- Instance health: host-level metrics show no immediate machine-level failure (EC2/hyperscaler reports OK).
- KV cache miss amplification: a subset of large multi-turn workloads exhausted local KV shards and caused high tail-latency while autoscaler spun up replacements. This produced request retries which magnified 502s.
- Autoscaler: increased nodes but observed slow replenishment (~6–8 min to restore pre-surge capacity) due to image pull / cold start for the large model variant.

Recommendation (one-step mitigation):
1) Immediate routing policy change: divert 60% of traffic from us-west-az2 to us-west-az1 and 40% to eastus2 for low-latency preservation. This avoids a full region failover and keeps most traffic within US latency SLOs. Estimated customer-perceived impact: brief connection resets for long-lived streams (~5–15s) as we drain.
2) Concurrent: spin a warm pool of model-backed instances with pre-warmed images (manual burst) in us-west-az2 to stabilise KV pressure. This requires exec approval for temporary capacity surge (expected 8 GPUs, ~2 hour window).

I can implement the routing weights now if Marcus approves. Playbook reference: manual-routing-playbook.pdf (excerpt attached).

Hannah
Hannah Schmitt
Principal SRE, Redwood Inference
hannah.schmitt@redwood.com
---
Attachments: manual-routing-playbook.pdf (pdf, 120 KB)

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Elena Costa <elena.costa@cobaltmetrics.com>, support@cobaltmetrics.com
Cc: Hannah Schmitt <hannah.schmitt@redwood.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 25 Jul 2028 18:33:05 -07:00
Subject: RE: High latency + 502 spike in us-west-az2 (production) — mitigation and next steps

Elena — quick update and actions we've taken today. Thanks again for the sample logs and patience.

What we did (timeline):
- 09:30 PT: Implemented weighted routing (60% -> us-west-az1, 40% -> eastus2) to reduce load on us-west-az2 and prevent global retry amplification.
- 09:35 PT: Activated pre-warmed instance pool in us-west-az2 (8 GPUs) to address KV cache pressure; observed gradual reduction in tail latency over the next hour.
- 11:15 PT: Observed stable throughput recovering to ~95% of baseline; 5xx rate reduced to <0.5% over 1 hour.

Current status: throughput and error rates are back within normal bounds for the last 4 hours. We continue to monitor for KV miss patterns during peak windows and will keep the warm pool active through tomorrow's peak.

Next steps (customer-visible & internal):
- We'll keep weighted routing in place for the rest of the day and begin a controlled return to original weights at 0200 PT overnight.
- We'll run an RCA and share an executive summary by EOD tomorrow.
- We propose a one-time service credit for the incident (we will surface the proposed credit amount separately) and can open a dedicated post-incident bridge if you'd like further granularity.

If you'd like an exec-to-exec update, Ethan Park (cc'd) is available this afternoon for a 20-min sync.

Elena, please let us know if you need additional logs or want the bridge recording. We're sorry for the impact and appreciate the quick flag.

Marcus
Marcus Lin
Senior Customer Reliability Manager, Redwood Inference
marcus.lin@redwood.com
---
Attachments: none

messages:
From: Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood-inference.com>
To: Avery Cole <avery.cole@calyxhealth.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood-inference.com>
Date: Wed, 15 May 2028 09:02:00 -0700
Subject: Calyx Health — on‑prem package & private networking gating plan

Hi Avery —

Thanks again for selecting Redwood for your private deployment. I'm handing this over from CS into implementation and want to lock the gating checklist so we can schedule the appliance shipment and VPC peering window. Proposed kickoff items (30–45 min):

- Confirm connectivity choice: AWS VPC peering vs. PrivateLink (endpoint) and preferred CIDR ranges.
- On‑prem package manifest: rack requirements, NICs, storage, and HSM/KMS placement.
- Firewall/NAT rules for egress to Redwood control plane & telemetry endpoints.
- Cert exchange and MTLS expectations.
- Timeline: network cutover & acceptance window (we prefer a 2‑hour early morning slot).

Attachments: calyx_network_requirements.xlsx (starter), redwood_onprem_manifest_v2.yaml (hardware manifest).

Can you share (a) the team we should loop for the peering; (b) any compliance locks (IP ranges we cannot touch); and (c) two 1‑hour options for next week for the kickoff? If you prefer, we can do a short pre‑call with your network engineer to validate the manifest before the group kickoff.

Thanks — I’ve cc'd Monica (network lead) and Ethan (on‑prem install PM) to own the delivery details.

— Logan
Logan Wright
Senior Customer Success Engineer | Redwood Inference
logan.wright@redwood-inference.com | +1 (415) 555-0118

From: Avery Cole <avery.cole@calyxhealth.com>
To: Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood-inference.com>
Date: Wed, 15 May 2028 10:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Calyx Health — on‑prem package & private networking gating plan

Thanks, Logan.

Appreciate the checklist — this looks like the right starting point. Our network lead (cc'd) is the Calyx DevOps alias (devops@calyxhealth.com). A few quick pieces up front:

- We prefer AWS VPC peering for the first phase; we may evaluate PrivateLink for the next region.
- Allowed CIDR space: 10.12.0.0/20 for our VPCs — we cannot change the /20 block.
- Compliance note: traffic logs must be retained for 2 years per our security policy; we’ll need to agree on a retention export before cutover.

Available kickoff windows (PDT):
- Tue 21 May 2028 08:00–09:00
- Wed 22 May 2028 11:00–12:00

I’ll follow up from the devops alias with our initial firewall export and contact for cert handling.

Best,
Avery Cole
Head of Integrations | Calyx Health
avery.cole@calyxhealth.com

From: Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood-inference.com>
To: Calyx DevOps <devops@calyxhealth.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood-inference.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood-inference.com>, Avery Cole <avery.cole@calyxhealth.com>
Date: Wed, 15 May 2028 12:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Calyx Health — on‑prem package & private networking gating plan

Hi team —

Looping in the devops alias — thanks for the quick reply, Avery. A few clarifications and asks so we can make the kickoff productive:

1) Peering details
   - If we proceed with VPC peering we will need: peer VPC ID, primary route table IDs, and the exact AZs you plan to use for the initial service endpoints.
   - Please confirm if you need BGP (transit) or if static route advertisement is sufficient. Redwood uses static route announces for most customer peering; we support BGP for advanced topologies.

2) CIDR and IP planning
   - Noted: 10.12.0.0/20. We'll propose a small /28 for the appliance endpoints. Can you confirm if you have a preallocated /28 for third‑party services or if we should plan to consume from the /20?

3) On‑prem manifest & hardware
   - Attached manifest (redwood_onprem_manifest_v2.yaml) lists NIC mapping, recommended BIOS settings, and HSM placement notes. Please review and flag any restrictions (e.g., locked HSM racks).

4) Security & certs
   - We will exchange an intermediate cert for the on‑prem appliance and require MTLS for control plane calls. Please confirm your preferred cert signing flow (ACME vs CSR signed by your CA).

5) Cutover window suggestions
   - We can do Tue 21 May 08:00–09:00 PDT. Monica will lead the network runbook and Ethan will own the install acceptance checklist.

Attachments: redwood_onprem_manifest_v2.yaml, calyx_network_requirements.xlsx
Docs: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1AbCdEfGhIjKlMnOp (shared runbook template)

If you can drop the firewall export prior to the kickoff that will save time during the run.

Onward —
— Logan

>

> - Tue 21 May 2028 08:00–09:00
> - Wed 22 May 2028 11:00–12:00
>
> I’ll follow up from the devops alias with our initial firewall export and contact for cert handling.
>
> Best,
> Avery

Date: Thu, 16 May 2028 08:20:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Calyx Health — on‑prem package & private networking gating plan

Logan / Monica / Ethan —

Thanks, confirming Tue 21 May 08:00–09:00 PDT. Quick technical items attached and included below:

- We will provide the peer VPC ID and route table after the change request is scheduled (expect to share by EOD Fri 17 May).
- Firewall export attached: calyx_fw_rules_v0.3.pdf. Key egress rules: allow 52.9.0.0/16 and 34.80.0.0/15 to TCP 443 and 5222 (control + telemetry).
- Cert flow: we will sign a CSR from our CA (internal PKI). Please provide the CSR template and required SANs.

Attachments: calyx_fw_rules_v0.3.pdf

Regards,
Calyx DevOps

Also attaching a short primer on our change windows and contact escalation matrix. If there are any dependencies on our side (HSM install or rack access), we will need a 3‑day notice prior to the shipment arrival.

Attachments: calyx_fw_rules_v0.3.pdf

Regards,
Calyx DevOps
devops@calyxhealth.com

On Wed, 15 May 2028 at 12:05 PM Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwood-inference.com> wrote:
> Hi team —
>
> Looping in the devops alias — thanks for the quick reply, Avery. A few clarifications and asks so we can make the kickoff productive:
>
> 1) Peering details
>    - If we proceed with VPC peering we will need: peer VPC ID, primary route table IDs, and the exact AZs you plan to use for the initial service endpoints.
>    - Please confirm if you need BGP (transit) or if static route advertisement is sufficient. Redwood uses static route announces for most customer peering; we support BGP for advanced topologies.
>
> 2) CIDR and IP planning
>    - Noted: 10.12.0.0/20. We'll propose a small /28 for the appliance endpoints. Can you confirm if you have a preallocated /28 for third‑party services or if we should plan to consume from the /20?
>
> 3) On‑prem manifest & hardware
>    - Attached manifest (redwood_onprem_manifest_v2.yaml) lists NIC mapping, recommended BIOS settings, and HSM placement notes. Please review and flag any restrictions (e.g., locked HSM racks).
>
> 4) Security & certs
>    - We will exchange an intermediate cert for the on‑prem appliance and require MTLS for control plane calls. Please confirm your preferred cert signing flow (ACME vs CSR signed by your CA).
>
> 5) Cutover window suggestions
>    - We can do Tue 21 May 08:00–09:00 PDT. Monica will lead the network runbook and Ethan will own the install acceptance checklist.
>
> Attachments: redwood_onprem_manifest_v2.yaml, calyx_network_requirements.xlsx
> Docs: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1AbCdEfGhIjKlMnOp (shared runbook template)
>
> If you can drop the firewall export prior to the kickoff that will save time during the run.
>

source.subject: Deal architecture: list-price offset, implementation credit, and reseller commission path
messages:
From: Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.ai>
To: Samir Gupta <samir@nebulahealth.com>
Date: 2028-03-25T09:14:00-07:00
Subject: Deal architecture: list-price offset, implementation credit, and reseller commission path

Samir —

Thanks again for the demo yesterday. Per our conversation you asked for a concise way to present discounts on the Azure/GCP marketplace while still providing an implementation credit that Nebula can apply against professional services. High level proposal from our side: 

- List-price offset (marketplace private offer): we show a reduced list price on the private offer so end customers see the discount on their marketplace invoice. This is implemented as a private-offer list-price override.
- Implementation credit: issued separately as a one-time credit (credit note / invoice credit) tied to the implementation SOW. It is not baked into the product line item.
- Reseller commission path: commission to Nebula's reseller will be calculated on product ARR (list-price less marketplace fee). Implementation credit will be excluded from product revenue calculation so it does not suppress partner commissions.

I've attached a mutual action plan (MAP) and a draft SOW that follows this flow. If that aligns with your procurement team we'll produce an updated private-offer PDF for signature and a sample invoice.

Next steps I'd suggest: 
1) Confirm procurement contact for PO flow and invoice sample requirements.
2) Confirm whether the implementation credit needs a separate PO line or can be an off-invoice credit.

Attaching: Nebula_MUTUAL_ACTION_PLAN.xlsx, Implementation_SOW_draft.pdf

Thanks —
Ethan Park
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ethan@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0198

From: Samir Gupta <samir@nebulahealth.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-03-26T10:02:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Deal architecture: list-price offset, implementation credit, and reseller commission path

Ethan —

Thanks for the clear summary. A couple quick clarifications from procurement and finance POV: 

- How will the implementation credit appear on our marketplace invoice? Will it be a separate credit line issued by Redwood or a contra line against the product? Our finance team requires the credit to be identifiable for audit.
- Does issuing an implementation credit affect the partner commission calculation? Our distributor expects to be paid on the net after marketplace fees, but they asked whether credits reduce their commission.

If you can send a sample (redacted) invoice and a proposed PO line-item structure that shows the credit as separate, that will speed things up. Forwarding procurement template attached.

Samir
Chief Product Officer | Nebula Health
samir@nebulahealth.com
procurement@nebulahealth.com

On Mon, Mar 25, 2028 at 9:14 AM Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Samir —
> 
> Thanks again for the demo yesterday... [message truncated]

From: Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.ai>
To: Irene Choi <irene_choi@redwood.ai>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa_ortiz@redwood.ai>
Cc: Samir Gupta <samir@nebulahealth.com>
Date: 2028-03-27T11:30:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: Deal architecture: list-price offset, implementation credit, and reseller commission path

Team —

Forwarding Samir's procurement questions (below). They want the implementation credit visible on the invoice for audit and want clarity on whether that reduces reseller commission. Can you confirm the recommended operational approach and whether we need to alter the MAP/SOW?

Quick asks: 
- Irene: accounting/revenue recognition implications and recommended invoice flow. Should the credit be a separate credit note?
- Vanessa: partner ops — confirm commission calculus and whether we should route commission on gross product revenue or after credits.

Samir, cc'd — we'll circle back with the ops approach and a sample invoice.

Thanks —
Ethan

On Tue, Mar 26, 2028 at 10:02 AM Samir Gupta <samir@nebulahealth.com> wrote:
> Ethan —
> 
> Thanks for the clear summary. A couple quick clarifications from procurement and finance POV... [message truncated]

From: Irene Choi <irene_choi@redwood.ai>
To: Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.ai>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa_ortiz@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-03-28T14:15:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Deal architecture: list-price offset, implementation credit, and reseller commission path

Ethan/Vanessa —

Recommended approach from a revenue and audit standpoint: 

1) Implementation credit as separate credit note: issue a one-time credit note (credit memos) tied to the SOW/invoice. This keeps product revenue and credits distinct in the ledger and satisfies auditor requirements.
2) Product revenue recognition: record product ARR at the private-offer list price (i.e., the list-price override that appears on the marketplace). The implementation credit should be accounted for as a professional services discount/credit and applied to the invoice total, not as an adjustment to the product unit price.
3) Commission calculation: standard practice is partner commission on product revenue (list-price) less marketplace transaction fees. Credits issued against professional services should not reduce partner commission on product ARR. If the credit is structured as a direct rebate against product line item, we would need to treat it as contra-revenue which would affect commission.

Operational notes: ensure the credit memo references the original invoice and SOW id; include GL codes in the credit documentation. I uploaded a sample SOW/credit flow template to the linked MAP.

Irene Choi
Revenue Operations Manager | Redwood Inference
irene_choi@redwood.ai

Attachments: Implementation_SOW_draft.pdf

From: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa_ortiz@redwood.ai>
To: Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.ai>, Irene Choi <irene_choi@redwood.ai>, Samir Gupta <samir@nebulahealth.com>
Date: 2028-04-03T16:42:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Deal architecture: list-price offset, implementation credit, and reseller commission path

Samir / Ethan / Irene —

Operational confirmation from partner/commission side: 

- Commission routing: we will pay Nebula's reseller commission on product revenue calculated using the private-offer list price minus marketplace transaction fee. That preserves consistent partner economics and avoids underpaying commission when implementation credits are used.
- Implementation credit treatment: Redwood will issue the implementation credit as a separate credit memo linked to the SOW. The credit memo will appear on the invoice as a distinct line and will not be applied as a contra to the product line item.
- In practical terms: partner commission team will receive the product invoice lines and compute commission on product gross; finance will receive the credit memo for professional services credits.

Next steps: Ethan, I've updated the MAP to reflect these items and attached an updated MAP (Updated_MAP_20280403.xlsx). Ethan / Samir — please confirm if Nebula wants the implementation credit as a separate PO/line (preferred) or if procurement will accept an off-invoice credit. Once confirmed we'll publish the updated private offer and sample invoice.

Best —
Vanessa Ortiz
Partner Operations | Redwood Inference
vanessa_ortiz@redwood.ai

--
Attachments: Updated_MAP_20280403.xlsx

From: Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.ai>
To: Samir Gupta <samir@nebulahealth.com>, procurement@nebulahealth.com
Cc: Irene Choi <irene_choi@redwood.ai>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa_ortiz@redwood.ai>
Date: 2028-04-03T16:47:00-07:00
Subject: Final: list-price offset + implementation credit flow (summary and next steps)

Samir / Procurement team —

Thanks for the patience. Quick summary and next steps based on the ops and rev-ops guidance above: 

Summary decisions:
- Private offer: list-price override on marketplace. Product line shows discounted list price.
- Implementation credit: issued as a separate credit memo (references SOW/invoice). Appears on invoice as its own line.
- Partner commission: reseller commission calculated on product revenue (private-offer list price) minus marketplace fee. Implementation credit will not reduce product commission.

Action items:
1) Samir / procurement: confirm whether you want the implementation credit issued as a separate PO/line item (preferred) or will accept an off-invoice credit.
2) Redwood: we'll generate a sample invoice and the private-offer PDF for signature once we have procurement preference.
3) Finance/Partner ops: we'll route commission reconciliation and credit memos to Nebula's AP contact for matching.

I've attached the updated MAP and the draft SOW. If that matches your team's needs we'll prepare the PO paperwork and DocuSign.

Thanks —
Ethan Park
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ethan@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0198

Attachments: Nebula_MUTUAL_ACTION_PLAN.xlsx, Updated_MAP_20280403.xlsx

source.subject: Dedicated capacity offer — floor/burst bands & phased activation
messages:
From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Derek Liu <derek@pathfinderbio.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2028-03-22T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Dedicated capacity offer — floor/burst bands & phased activation

Derek —

Thanks again for the call earlier today. Per our conversation, I attached a concise proposal that outlines three commitment profiles for a dedicated GPU pool tailored to your mixed workload (retrieval-augmented generation + embedding heavy indexing + nightly bulk batches). High level:

- Option A — Conservative floor: 8 reserved A100-equivalent GPUs, warm pool for burst to 12 for 3 hours/day; activation window month 1.
- Option B — Balanced ramp: start at 12 reserved, 6-week ramp to 24 reserved with predictable monthly increments; 40% burst headroom for 2-hour windows.
- Option C — Growth ladder: 24 reserved baseline with scheduled scale-up lanes to 48 during peak training/classification cycles (requires 45-day lead).

Key considerations included in the PDF: min-GPU SLA (99.9 availability on baseline), warm pool sizing, burst lanes, activation lead times, and recommended quantization & batching profiles per workload to hit your cost targets.

Action items for you:
- Tell us which commitment profile you prefer (A/B/C) or request a hybrid.
- Confirm the two models you'll primarily serve (embedding model name + chat/generation model).
- Any procurement constraints or preferred billing cadence.

Attached: DedCap_Proposal_Pathfinder_v1.pdf, GPU_Runbook_Pathfinder.xlsx

Happy to hop on another 30-min session if you want to walk the numbers.

Best,
Ethan Park
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ethan.park@redwoodinference.com | +1 (415) 555-0133

---

From: Derek Liu <derek@pathfinderbio.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Anna Kim <anna@pathfinderbio.com>
Date: 2028-03-23T11:54:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity offer — floor/burst bands & phased activation

Ethan —

Appreciate the quick turnaround and the clean breakdown. A few clarifying questions before we pick a lane:

1) For Option B (balanced ramp) — can you confirm the warm pool cost delta versus baseline? We want predictable run-rate into Q3.
2) For Option C — what are the lead time risks if we need to accelerate scale from 24 to 48 in 30 days instead of 45?
3) SLA: does the 99.9 availability apply per-region or per-customer instance? We plan to split inference across two regions later in the year.

Also, curious whether you recommend pinning model versions for the first 6 months to avoid rollout regressions. We can share the model names next week.

Can Marissa get a quick pricing grid so Procurement can review?

Thanks,
Derek Liu
CTO, Pathfinder Bio
derek@pathfinderbio.com

---

From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Derek Liu <derek@pathfinderbio.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2028-03-23T14:02:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity offer — floor/burst bands & phased activation

Derek —

Answers inline:

1) Warm pool cost delta (Option B): warm pool capacity is billed at a 30% discounted warm-rate vs fully reserved GPUs because we keep them in a pre-warmed state (short spin-up). In the proposal PDF we show example burn rates at expected utilization slices (20%/50%/80%). For your Q3 planning, assume a +15% monthly run-rate over pure reserved baseline when using the warm pool for predictable daily 2-hour spikes.

2) Lead time risk for Option C: accelerating to 30 days is possible but contingent on available cloud inventory in the target AZs. We can provision cross-AZ burst lanes to mitigate some risk, but that adds networking & cross-region egress considerations (we call it "geo-bursting" in the runbook). If you need hard guarantees for a 30-day accelerate, we recommend locking a short-term capacity buffer up front (small add-on commit) to reduce preemption risk.

3) SLA scope: 99.9% is per-customer capacity pool in a region (so separate pools would have independent SLAs). We'll include fallback routing if a region drops below SLO — traffic can failover to the secondary pool with model-compatible fallbacks.

On model pinning: yes, we recommend pinning major model families while you ramp (first 3–6 months), then enabling phased rollouts with canaries and automatic rollback tied to output-quality monitoring. We'll bake that into the rollout policy in the console.

Marissa — can you share a compact pricing grid (monthly cost bands + one-time activation fees + sample run-rate scenarios) that I can attach to the PDF? Karthik, feel free to add any infra notes about quantization or batch/kv-cache settings that materially change GPU requirement projections.

Thanks —
Ethan

---

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
To: Derek Liu <derek@pathfinderbio.com>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2028-03-24T09:08:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity offer — floor/burst bands & phased activation

Derek —

Attached is a one-page pricing grid (also pasted below for quick scan). This reflects committed monthly charges, one-time activation, and examples of expected token-hour burn at three utilization profiles.

Pricing summary (examples):
- Option A (8 baseline): $42,000/mo committed + $6,500 one-time activation; warm-burst overage billed at usage; typical monthly run-rate at 30% utilization: $18k.
- Option B (12 -> 24 over 6 weeks): starting $63,000/mo; ramp discount (applied weeks 3–6) reduces avg monthly by ~8% in months 1–3; one-time activation $9,500. Warm pool add-on: $3,200/mo.
- Option C (24 baseline): $118,000/mo committed; activation $18,000; recommended buffer (optional) for 30-day accelerate: $12,000/mo.

Terms highlights: 12-month minimum preferred, month-to-month available with 60-day notice for downscale. Billing cadence monthly with net-30. Procurement asks: legal wants a clause on capacity migration and retained KV cache behavior on failover; we'll provide standard language and a short tech appendix.

Attachments: DedCap_Proposal_Pathfinder_v1.pdf (updated with grid), GPU_Runbook_Pathfinder.xlsx

If that looks OK, I can kick off a formal quote and a DocuSign packet (deal id PF-2028-03-01).

Best,
Marissa Cole
Senior Account Executive, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com | +1 (415) 555-0199

Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity offer — floor/burst bands & phased activation

We want to proceed with a hybrid of Option B but with a slightly accelerated ramp: start at 14 reserved GPUs, scale to 28 in 5 weeks, warm-pool set to allow bursts to 40 for 2 hours/day. Derek

source.subject: Deployment Fencing Evidence: consolidated handbook and next-step asks
messages:
From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Luis Fernandez <luis.fernandez@veridian.com>, enterprise_audit@veridian.com
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, 1 Dec 2027 08:15:00 -0800
Subject: Deployment Fencing Evidence: consolidated handbook and next-step asks

Hi Luis / Veridian audit team —

Thanks again for the time yesterday. Per our call you asked for a compact, single-reference packet that explains how Redwood separates and protects customer workloads across Hosted / Dedicated / Private deployment modes, with an emphasis on the network isolation and egress controls that underpin SOC 2 / SIG control assertions.

I’m attaching a short handbook (redwood_fencing_diagram_v2.pdf) and a control-to-evidence matrix (tenant_fencing_matrix.xlsx). The handbook contains:
- a concise narrative for each deployment mode (Hosted API, Dedicated reserved pools, Private VPC/on-prem)
- annotated network diagrams showing control-plane vs data-plane boundaries, peering/egress demarcation, and optional air-gap patterns for highly regulated customers
- evidence pointers (logs, config exports, kms key references) mapped to SIG/CIS/SOC2 controls

Immediate asks from your side so we can target the follow-up bundle:
1) Please confirm which control families you want prioritized (network segmentation, egress control, key management, and audit trails were discussed).
2) If you have a preferred format for certificate/artefact delivery (single ZIP vs multiple signed Drive links vs direct S3 share).
3) Any specific diagram view you need expanded (east-west ACLs, NAT gateway configs, or edge termination cert chains).

Notes on items included in this initial packet:
- Network diagrams are annotated with VLAN/subnet ranges masked for customer privacy; we can provide redacted config exports on request.
- Dedicated: shows tenant GPU pool isolation, dedicated VPCs per tenant with either routed peering or private interconnect options.
- Private: includes guidance for on-prem packaging and the optional air-gapped control-plane pattern used for restricted workloads.

If useful I can schedule a 30-minute review with our architecture team (Monica and Rishi) next week to walk through the diagrams.

Thanks —
Ethan Park
Head of Solutions — Security Programs
Redwood Inference

Attachments: redwood_fencing_diagram_v2.pdf, tenant_fencing_matrix.xlsx
From: Luis Fernandez <luis.fernandez@veridian.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>, enterprise_audit@veridian.com
Date: Wed, 1 Dec 2027 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Deployment Fencing Evidence: consolidated handbook and next-step asks

Ethan —

Thanks for the quick turnaround and the packet. Initial read-through looks good; high level feedback:
- We’ll prioritize network segmentation (SIG Scoping) and egress controls first. Those are gating items for our infra assessment.
- Delivery: prefer a signed ZIP sent to our secure intake address (enterprise_audit@veridian.com) for audit ingestion.
- For diagrams we’ll need an expanded view showing ACLs and the exact NAT/egress gateway pattern you use for Dedicated (we need to validate that tenant traffic does not transit shared control-plane subnets).

Also two clarifying questions:
1) Can you provide the KMS key rotation policy for Dedicated-managed keys vs customer-managed keys in Private? (policy doc or last rotation timestamp is fine).
2) For your hosted offering: how are ephemeral workloads sandboxed to prevent cross-tenant KV-cache leakage? We flagged that as a potential risk area.

If you can drop the signed ZIP by Friday we’ll get it into the queue and confirm the intake ticket.

—Luis
Luis Fernandez
Security Architect, Veridian Financial
luis.fernandez@veridian.com
From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>, Luis Fernandez <luis.fernandez@veridian.com>, enterprise_audit@veridian.com
Date: Thu, 2 Dec 2027 10:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Deployment Fencing Evidence: consolidated handbook and next-step asks

Hi team — quick updates against Luis’s asks: 

1) ACL / NAT detail: attached I’ve included network_annotation.svg which expands the Dedicated diagram to show the egress NAT GW placement, per-tenant NAT instances (when configured), and the control-plane transit restrictions. In Dedicated the tenant inference traffic uses isolated ENIs tied to a tenant VPC; control-plane RPCs (model management, scheduling) are routed over an isolated management VPC with strict firewall rules — there is no direct path from tenant data-plane subnets into other tenants’ data-plane subnets.

2) KMS rotation: we maintain a company key-rotation policy (rotations every 90 days) for Redwood-managed keys; for Private deployments we document the option and process for customer-managed CMKs (we can provide a last-rotation timestamp for the key used in your PoC if you want). I’ll include a short export from our internal KMS audit as part of the ZIP.

3) Ephemeral sandboxing / KV-cache leakage: our inference runtime implements per-request KV isolation and enforces strict cache scoping at the process boundary. Practically that means KV caches are namespaced by tenant id and stored in memory segments that are torn down after GRACE window; we also have runtime assertions and unit tests that validate cache boundaries. I can include the test harness summary and a sample trace showing cache flush events.

Next steps — we plan to assemble a signed ZIP (soc2_share_packet.zip) that contains: the handbook PDF, the expanded SVG, the control matrix, KMS audit export, and a concise test-harness summary.

Luis — do you want the raw ACL snippets (cloud provider JSON) included or prefer them redacted/abstracted?

—Monica
Monica Patel
Senior Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference
From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Luis Fernandez <luis.fernandez@veridian.com>, enterprise_audit@veridian.com
Date: Thu, 2 Dec 2027 14:45:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Deployment Fencing Evidence: consolidated handbook and next-step asks

Thanks Monica — good contents. A couple of governance/packaging notes from my side:
- For intake we should produce two ZIP variants: (A) redacted artefact set (no explicit subnet or IP ranges) for sharing with wide audit groups, and (B) full artefact set for Veridian's secure intake only (enterprise_audit@veridian.com). Label each ZIP with a manifest file that lists included items and hash checks.
- Add a README.txt inside the ZIP that maps each artefact to SIG/SOC2 control IDs (so reviewers can find evidence quickly). Monica — can you author that mapping? I’ll sign the ZIPs once assembled.

Luis — we’ll aim to deliver redacted ZIP to your inbox by EOD Friday and the full ZIP to enterprise_audit@veridian.com once we have confirmation of the secure intake path.

—Kimberly
Kimberly Park
Director, Compliance Programs, Redwood Inference
From: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Luis Fernandez <luis.fernandez@veridian.com>, enterprise_audit@veridian.com
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 3 Dec 2027 16:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Deployment Fencing Evidence: consolidated handbook and next-step asks

Luis / audit team — thanks for the direction. Summary of commitments: 
- We will deliver two ZIPs (redacted and full) as Kimberly described.
- The full package (soc2_share_packet.zip) will be uploaded to our secure S3 share and a signed download link pushed to enterprise_audit@veridian.com.
- Included evidence: redwood_fencing_diagram_v2.pdf, network_annotation.svg, tenant_fencing_matrix.xlsx, KMS audit extract, runtime test-harness summary, and manifest + checksums.

Action items:
- Luis: please confirm secure intake S3 URL or confirm enterprise_audit@veridian.com is the correct recipient for the full package.

Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, 1 Dec 2027 08:15:00 -0800
Subject: Deployment Fencing Evidence: consolidated handbook and next-step asks

Hi Luis / Veridian audit team —

Thanks again for the time yesterday. Per our call you asked for a compact, single-reference packet that explains how Redwood separates and protects customer workloads across Hosted / Dedicated / Private deployment modes, with an emphasis on the network isolation and egress controls that underpin SOC 2 / SIG control assertions.

I’m attaching a short handbook (redwood_fencing_diagram_v2.pdf) and a control-to-evidence matrix (tenant_fencing_matrix.xlsx). The handbook contains:
- a concise narrative for each deployment mode (Hosted API, Dedicated reserved pools, Private VPC/on-prem)
- annotated network diagrams showing control-plane vs data-plane boundaries, peering/egress demarcation, and optional air-gap patterns for highly regulated customers
- evidence pointers (logs, config exports, kms key references) mapped to SIG/CIS/SOC2 controls

Immediate asks from your side so we can target the follow-up bundle:
1) Please confirm which control families you want prioritized (network segmentation, egress control, key management, and audit trails were discussed).
2) If you have a preferred format for certificate/artefact delivery (single ZIP vs multiple signed Drive links vs direct S3 share).
3) Any specific diagram view you need expanded (east-west ACLs, NAT gateway configs, or edge termination cert chains).

Notes on items included in this initial packet:
- Network diagrams are annotated with VLAN/subnet ranges masked for customer privacy; we can provide redacted config exports on request.
- Dedicated: shows tenant GPU pool isolation, dedicated VPCs per tenant with either routed peering or private interconnect options.
- Private: includes guidance for on-prem packaging and the optional air-gapped control-plane pattern used for restricted workloads.

If useful I can schedule a 30-minute review with our architecture team (Monica and Rishi) next week to walk through the diagrams.

Thanks —
Ethan Park
Head of Solutions — Security Programs
Redwood Inference

Attachments: redwood_fencing_diagram_v2.pdf, tenant_fencing_matrix.xlsx


From: Luis Fernandez <luis.fernandez@veridian.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>, enterprise_audit@veridian.com
Date: Wed, 1 Dec 2027 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Deployment Fencing Evidence: consolidated handbook and next-step asks

Ethan —

Thanks for the quick turnaround and the packet. Initial read-through looks good; high level feedback:
- We’ll prioritize network segmentation (SIG Scoping) and egress controls first. Those are gating items for our infra assessment.
- Delivery: prefer a signed ZIP sent to our secure intake address (enterprise_audit@veridian.com) for audit ingestion.
- For diagrams we’ll need an expanded view showing ACLs and the exact NAT/egress gateway pattern you use for Dedicated (we need to validate that tenant traffic does not transit shared control-plane subnets).

Also two clarifying questions:
1) Can you provide the KMS key rotation policy for Dedicated-managed keys vs customer-managed keys in Private? (policy doc or last rotation timestamp is fine).
2) For your hosted offering: how are ephemeral workloads sandboxed to prevent cross-tenant KV-cache leakage? We flagged that as a potential risk area.

If you can drop the signed ZIP by Friday we’ll get it into the queue and confirm the intake ticket.

—Luis
Luis Fernandez
Security Architect, Veridian Financial
luis.fernandez@veridian.com


From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>, Luis Fernandez <luis.fernandez@veridian.com>, enterprise_audit@veridian.com
Date: Thu, 2 Dec 2027 10:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Deployment Fencing Evidence: consolidated handbook and next-step asks

Hi team — quick updates against Luis’s asks: 

1) ACL / NAT detail: attached I’ve included network_annotation.svg which expands the Dedicated diagram to show the egress NAT GW placement, per-tenant NAT instances (when configured), and the control-plane transit restrictions. In Dedicated the tenant inference traffic uses isolated ENIs tied to a tenant VPC; control-plane RPCs (model management, scheduling) are routed over an isolated management VPC with strict firewall rules — there is no direct path from tenant data-plane subnets into other tenants’ data-plane subnets.

2) KMS rotation: we maintain a company key-rotation policy (rotations every 90 days) for Redwood-managed keys; for Private deployments we document the option and process for customer-managed CMKs (we can provide a last-rotation timestamp for the key used in your PoC if you want). I’ll include a short export from our internal KMS audit as part of the ZIP.

3) Ephemeral sandboxing / KV-cache leakage: our inference runtime implements per-request KV isolation and enforces strict cache scoping at the process boundary. Practically that means KV caches are namespaced by tenant id and stored in memory segments that are torn down after GRACE window; we also have runtime assertions and unit tests that validate cache boundaries. I can include the test harness summary and a sample trace showing cache flush events.

Next steps — we plan to assemble a signed ZIP (soc2_share_packet.zip) that contains: the handbook PDF, the expanded SVG, the control matrix, KMS audit export, and a concise test-harness summary.

Luis — do you want the raw ACL snippets (cloud provider JSON) included or prefer them redacted/abstracted?

—Monica
Monica Patel
Senior Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference

—Kimberly

From: Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.com>
To: Sara Liao <sara.liao@medicahealth.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 01 Dec 2027 17:12:00 +0000
Subject: Cashflow commitment check-in — Invoice 9211

Hi Sara —

Hope you're well. Quick follow-up on Invoice 9211 (due 2027-12-05). We want to make sure you have the easiest route to settle: ACH, wire, or virtual card (if treasury prefers). If wire, I can loop in our finance team with the bank stub and target cutoff windows.

If there are any PO or approval hangups, can you share the expected payment date so we can align downstream entitlements? If you prefer, I can move this to an AP-only thread and include our accounts contact.

Thanks — appreciate the quick confirmation.

Best,
Ethan Park
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ethan@redwood.com
(415) 555-0192

Attachment: none

To: Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.com>
Cc: MedicaHealth Billing <payments@medicahealth.com>
Date: Thu, 02 Dec 2027 09:05:00 -0500
Subject: Re: Cashflow commitment check-in — Invoice 9211

Hi Ethan —

Thanks for the ping. We're targeting payment early next week, but Treasury asked for a few clarifications before they schedule the wire:

- Can you confirm Redwood's wire instructions (SWIFT + ABA or IBAN) so they can set up the outbound transfer?
- Our AP team flagged VAT handling: do you have an EU VAT invoice variant or evidence of tax treatment we can attach? We're reconciling across entities.
- PO attached (PO_8793.pdf) — is the invoice mapping OK for reconciliation?

If we can get the bank stub and a VAT note, they should be able to push the wire on 2027-12-03 (company cutoffs permitting). If a virtual card is faster, we can explore that too.

Sara
Sara Liao
Senior AP Manager, MedicaHealth
sara.liao@medicahealth.com
Attachment: PO_8793.pdf (PDF, 34KB)

To: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Sara Liao <sara.liao@medicahealth.com>, MedicaHealth Billing <payments@medicahealth.com>
Date: Thu, 02 Dec 2027 09:20:00 +0000
Subject: Fwd: Cashflow commitment check-in — Invoice 9211

Laura / Ben — forwarding Sara's note from MedicaHealth. Can you confirm the bank wire stub (ABA/SWIFT) and whether we can produce a VAT statement or EU-friendly invoice PDF? PO is attached on their side.

If Treasury needs a billing contact for a quick phone auth, I'm happy to jump on the call — they mentioned a potential 12/03 wire.

From: Sara Liao <sara.liao@medicahealth.com>
Date: Thu, 02 Dec 2027 09:05:00 -0500
Subject: Re: Cashflow commitment check-in — Invoice 9211
To: Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.com>
Cc: MedicaHealth Billing <payments@medicahealth.com>

[see original message and attachment PO_8793.pdf]

----------

Ethan

Attachment: none
From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Sara Liao <sara.liao@medicahealth.com>, Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 02 Dec 2027 10:00:00 -0000
Subject: Re: Cashflow commitment check-in — Invoice 9211 (wire details + VAT note)

Hi Sara —

Thanks for the heads-up. Bank details and a copy of Invoice 9211 are attached. Quick points:

- For US wires: ABA 021000021, Account 412345678 (Redwood Inference, Inc.)
- For international wires: Bank of Westbridge, SWIFT BWSTUS33, IBAN: US00BWST000412345678
- ACH is also accepted (Account: 412345678, Routing: 021000021) — please note ACH settlement is typically T+1.
- Cutoff for same-day credit (wire) is 17:00 ET for most US banks; SWIFT settlement depends on intermediary banks.

VAT/tax: Redwood is a US-headquartered entity and does not issue EU VAT-charged invoices. We can provide a tax-exempt statement and a billing PDF that shows net amounts and our US tax id for your reconciliation. If MedicaHealth needs a specific VAT form, let us know the required fields and we'll generate a tailored invoice PDF.

I've attached: bank_details.pdf (bank stub with ABA/SWIFT) and invoice_9211.pdf (billing PDF).

If Treasury wants to route via virtual card, we can provide the virtual-card payment link (one-time charge) — that clears faster but may have a small processing fee.

Ben Carter
Payments Ops Lead, Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.com
Attachment: bank_details.pdf (PDF, 8KB); invoice_9211.pdf (PDF, 42KB)
From: Sara Liao <sara.liao@medicahealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Ethan Park <ethan@redwood.com>
Cc: MedicaHealth Billing <payments@medicahealth.com>
Date: Thu, 02 Dec 2027 13:30:00 -0500
Subject: Re: Cashflow commitment check-in — Invoice 9211

Ben / Ethan —

Perfect, thanks for the stub and the clarification on VAT. Treasury confirmed they will schedule an outgoing wire on 2027-12-03 and will include invoice_9211.pdf for reconciliation. We'll send a remittance email once the transfer is initiated.

If anything changes on our end I will update you, otherwise expect confirmation by Friday morning. Appreciate the quick turnaround.

Best,
Sara
Sara Liao
Senior AP Manager, MedicaHealth
Attachment: none
